text: Environmental Goals and Sustainable Prosperity Act (EGSPA)
4 (1) The long-term environmental and economic objective of the Province is to fully
integrate environmental sustainability and economic prosperity and to this end to
(a) demonstrate international leadership by having one of the cleanest and
most sustainable environments in the world by the year 2020; and
(b) provide certainty to all sectors of the economy through the Government's
economic development strategy entitled Opportunities for Sustainable
Prosperity and establish clear environmental goals while improving the
Province's economic performance to a level that is equal to or above the
The government gauges progress of the Nova Scotia economy over time and how its place
changes within the Canadian context. Environmental and economic progress have complex
Indicators were selected for each of the domains based on the following questions:
 What is it producing for us? How well are resources used?
A working group screened indicators on the basis of the following criteria:
 easily compared to Canada and other provinces
 make sense to the general public
A number of very significant challenges were encountered in identifying and selecting
• Data that meets the ideal is very limited
• Data is not usually updated every year
• Data can be 2-5 years (or more) out-of-date
• Important information is often collected at the provincial level and is difficult to
locate, compile and do cross-jurisdictional comparisons
• The most valid and useful indicators may be difficult for a layperson to interpret
The list of indicators presented below fall within the interconnected circles of environment,
economy, and society. Conventional economic indicators like GDP and consumption are
important, but they tell only part of the story. Supplementing economic indicators of
material prosperity with social and environmental indicators gives a fuller picture of well-
being. GDP was always intended to be an important, but not singular indicator of
 GDP does not take into account unpaid human efforts or contributions, such as
childcare or volunteer work, essential as they may be.
 It was not designed to measure any impact on asset stocks such as
 It does not consider whether economic activity is the direct result of negative
factors such as increased pollution, debt, crime, accidents or health problems.
Data on the environment, including natural resources, is collected by a number of separate
entities for very different purposes. Government will continue to develop broader
indicators of environmental and social well-being.
Nova Scotia’s individual These indicators provide a snapshot of the Nova progress -
Scotia economy in the Canadian context. The indicators are chosen to be used together, to
give context to the many economic forces at work, including the development of the social
and environmental aspects of the Nova Scotia economy. Considering the close-linked
nature of social, environmental, and economic sustainability
Indicators of success Linking results to programs and strategies (strategic planning) -
are chosen to measure the degree to which outcomes are achieved. Headline indicators
provide broad benchmark data which can be used to judge the well-being of the Nova
Scotia economy, and in reference to its place in Canada.
Economic growth and other strategies can make use of similar high-level indicators and
benchmarks from which to measure success.
At times, it may be difficult to Understanding the dynamics that influence indicators -
understand trends and underlying causes that drive the results we see. In addition to local
Nova Scotia’s economy and environment economic conditions, external forces influence
(as well as those in other Provinces). However, the structure of Provincial economies
evolves slowly in response to the complex interaction of both internal and external
influences. Comparisons between Nova Scotia and other Provinces must acknowledge the
varying degrees of sensitivity to global conditions and the relative speed with which
economies adapt to new market realities. These indicators provide a starting point from
“why is this or “what other factors are at work here?” which we can ask, happening?”,
The three domains chosen for the purposes of reporting are based on the following
principle: the health of the economy, the health of the environment and the health of the
Each indicator was categorized according to four criteria:
–  Level Without comparison to the rest of Canada, would the level be considered
 Trend over the past few years
For ease of interpretation, weather icons were assigned to each indicator:
Favorable level, high to middle Canadian ranking, overall positive trend
Favorable to neutral level, middle to low Canadian ranking, flat or positive trend
Neutral to unfavorable level, middle to low Canadian ranking, flat or negative
Unfavorable level, low Canadian ranking, flat or negative trend
overview of the province’s performance in key areas. This provides a simple at-a-glance

Individuals reporting three or more years of low income in the previous six years.
Source of Persistence of low income, by selected characteristics, every 3 years, Statistics Canada
Description of Based on the Survey of Labour and Income Dynamics.
Rationale for Persistence in low income is correlated with poor health, limited employment and life skills
selection and lower levels of income equality.
What it tells Many people face barriers to participation even when the economy is doing well, often the
us result of low levels of education and inadequate social supports.
Level: Lower than the Canadian average.
Rank: 3rd lowest in terms of percentage in Low Income (third best in Canada).
Trend: There was a downward trend (improvement) from 1996/2001 to 2005/2010 in Nova
Scotia, as with most provinces. The period between 96/01 and 02/05 saw deterioration in
some provinces, but all have shown improvement with the exception of Saskatchewan,
which showed a slight increase in the percentage of people in low income for three or more

Gini coefficients of after-tax income for economic families and unattached individuals,
“The Gini coefficient is a number between zero and one that measures the relative degree of Description of
Data inequality in the distribution of income. The coefficient would register zero (minimum
inequality) for a population in which each member received exactly the same income and it
would register a coefficient of one (maximum inequality) if one member received all the
income and the rest received none.” Statistics Canada
Rationale for Commonly used measures of economic growth will appear positive, even if economic
selection growth primarily benefits only a small percentage of the population. Higher levels of
income equality have been correlated with positive social and personal outcomes for all
What it tells Reflects the impact of changes in society and the economy on the distribution of income
Level: Level is better than the Canadian average.
– Rank: 2011 10th among provinces (behind NB, MB, and PE) 3rd among four Atlantic
Trend: Overall increase in inequality since mid 1990s, but with notable improvements in
Nova Scotia’s level of income inequality in early 2000s. 2011 saw an improvement over
Scotia’s Gini 2010 levels, which deteriorated from 2007-9. Nova Coefficient is the lowest
it’s been in over a decade; decreases in income inequality were seen in NS, NB, QUE, ON,
MB, and BC compared to 2001 levels.

Description of Percentage of respondents (age 12 and over) to Canada Community Health Survey who
Data described their sense of belonging to the local community as strong or very strong.
(no change in data since last update)
Rationale for Research shows a high correlation of sense of community-belonging with physical and
What it tells A variety of formal and informal social networks form the basis of social capital. Local
us communities are the fundamental level of social capital. This captures, at the local
community level, how inter-connected people perceive themselves to be.
Level: Sense of belonging is above the Canadian average. Perceived mental and physical
- Nova Scotia is ranked 7th in the country in terms of respondents who said they perceived
their physical health as “very good or excellent” (57.8%, a decline from the previous 58.5%
- Nova Scotia is ranked 5th in the country in terms of respondents who said they perceived
their mental health as “very good or excellent” (71.8%). This ranking is unchanged from last
time. All provinces with the exception of NB saw a decline in this percentage.
in terms of percentage of respondents who said they have “very - Nova Scotia is ranked 4th
strong or somewhat strong sense of belonging to local community.” This is a slight decline
Trend: Stable or steady. Trend is visibly declining across Canada.

Population with post-secondary education (age 24-44), Statistics Canada , Labour force
Description of Percentage of 25 to 44 year olds with completion of post-secondary certificate diploma or
Rationale for Educational attainment is related to average earnings, labour productivity, health, and
selection incidence of poverty. This indicator reflects trends in post-secondary choices and Nova
Scotia’s ability to retain highly skilled workers.
What it tells Almost two-thirds of 25 to 44 year-olds living in the province have completed a
us university, college, or trades apprenticeship program. Although our rates continue to
increase, we have fallen below the Canadian average and other provinces are moving
ahead of us in terms of developing and attracting a highly skilled younger working age
Level: 69.6% of 24-44 year olds in the Labour Force Population have post secondary
education in the province, a 0.2 percentage point change since 2010.
Rank: Nova Scotia is still ranked 4th among provinces (unchanged from 2012 to 2013),
and is at the Canadian average.
Trend: In 2013, NS remains at the national average and is ranked #4 n the country (PE and
AB gained places while BC and NB moved down in the rankings). There has been a
steady increase in this indicator over the last decade in Nova Scotia. In the year 2000, only
59.8% of 25-44 year olds in the LF Population had post secondary education.

Life expectancy in years at age 65.
Life expectancy, abridged life table, at age 65, Statistics Canada (2006) CANSIM Table
Description of Estimated years of life remaining at age 65.
Rationale for Life expectancy is related to overall health, levels of chronic disease, social supports, and
selection level of education. Life expectancy, as a reflection of overall health, has implications for
future health expenditures and labour market participation of older workers.
Although life expectancy at 65 is increasing, the rate of increase is slower than the Canadian
Level: Nova Scotia is below the national average (20.2 yrs) at 19.3 years of life expectancy
Rank: NS is ranked 8th among provinces, tied with PE, above NL, and below NB
Trend: Improving. Since 1996, NS has seen an improvement in life expectancy of 1.7 years,
above the 10 province average of 1.6 (and ranked #3 in terms of improvement over that
period), but not at the same rate as the rest of the provinces.

Employment rates of 25-44 year-olds (visible minority diversity groups) compared with
employment rate of 25-44 year-old population as a whole.
Source of Census of Canada, Statistics Canada, 2006.
Description of The employment rate for visible minorities is compared to the overall employment rate for a
Data specific age group (25-44) by province. (Does not include Aboriginal identifiers).
Data Every five years (Census), National Household Survey (2011 and after)
Rationale for Employment participation is related to literacy, levels of education, and the effectiveness of
selection informal and formal social supports. Barriers to full participation in the labour market have
been a longstanding issue for visible minorities. As the labour force gets smaller, barriers to
participation need to be addressed to create an employment climate that will be attractive to
What it tells Although below the average for the population of 25-44 year-olds as a whole, the
us employment rate of visible minorities has increased.
Level: In 2011, visible minorities (aged 25-44) had an employment rate of 68.5% whereas
the employment rate for the whole 25-44 year old cohort was 79.7%.
Rank: 7th among provinces in 2011. Both employment rates are below the Canadian average.
Trend: 2011 data cannot be used for trend analysis as the census was replaced with the
voluntary National Household Survey. Census 2016 may provide trend analysis ability.

Labour productivity growth in the business sector.
Source of Real Labour Productivity Growth in the Business Sector, (Chained (2007) dollars per hour),
Description of Labour productivity is the ratio between real value added and hours worked.
Rationale for Effective use of human capital, matching labour force skills to business requirements, is a
selection major factor in labour productivity growth. Labour productivity related to literacy and
educational attainment in the labour force and a business culture of competitive thinking and
What it tells The growth rate has fluctuated between positive and negative between 2008 and 2013.
us There has been an overall downward trend in labour productivity growth in the business
sector since 1999. Post recession data show recovery of labour productivity across Canada.
Level: Slightly below the Canadian average, which is skewed by provinces with higher
natural resource (oil/gas) export content. NS is more in line with Ontario and Quebec levels.
Rank: In 2013, Nova Scotia was ranked 3rdth among provinces, with productivity growth at
2.0%, marking a return to positive productivity gains. Productivity growth in NS has risen
from zero growth over the 2004-06 period and the 0.5% growth over the 2007-09 period.
Trend: In the last three years, NS has been ranked 3rd, 4th, and 3rd in labour productivity
growth (individual years, not average over a period).
Labour Productivity Growth in the Business Sector

Investment less depreciation for non-residential capital stock.
Flows and stocks of fixed non-residential Source of
Description of Annual investment in non-residential capital stock minus annual depreciation.
Rationale for Built capital assets support productive economic and social activities.
What it tells Ongoing investment in capital stock is required to maintain old stock and build new stock.
us Levels above zero imply that new capital stock is being built to meet ongoing and emerging
needs. Low investment levels imply that, not only is little new stock being built, but that
maintenance is being deferred on existing stock.
Rank: 10th among Canadian provinces (2012, 2013, and 11-13 average).
Trend: Flat but declining from levels of three years ago.

Selected economic indicators, provincial economic accounts, Statistics Canada (2008),
Rationale for Savings rates are used as a proxy for personal debt.
What it tells This indicator measures the combined impact of debt-based personal expenditures and an
us increasing percentage of retirees in the population, who are spending investment income.
These both potentially have an impact on personal funds available for investment.
Rank: 8th among provinces, well below the Canadian average of 4.8%.
Trend: Improving since 2008, and better than the 5-year average of -2.1%.

Provincial Economic Accounts, Expenditure Based GDP (Chained $2002)
Estimates of population, annual, Statistics Canada (2010), CANSIM Table 051-0001.
Description of Consumer spending is one part of the composition of provincial Gross Domestic Product.
Data Data is collected and reported on an annual basis from all Provinces.
Rationale for As a comparator to the Personal Savings Rate indicator, consumer spending trends indicate
selection how quickly personal expenditures rebound from recessions and grow provincial GDP. It
also indicates the relationship between provincial GDP per capita and its flow through to
What it tells Nova Scotia is ranked fifth in terms of consumer spending per capita, behind Alberta, BC,
us Ontario and Saskatchewan, and while the Canadian figure dipped overall in 2009, NS had
Level: Consumer spending per capita is $26,820, below the Canadian average of $27,563.
Trend: Increasing. 2009-10 growth is 3rd highest in the country (3.9% growth yr/yr).

Annual Gross domestic product (GDP) (chained), expenditure-based, provincial economic
Data accounts, Statistics Canada (2008), CANSIM Table 384-0002.
Estimates of population, annual, Statistics Canada (2009) CANSIM Table 051-0001.
Description of Represents the rate of growth or the entire annual economic output of the province.
Rationale for A well-known measure of economic output.
What it tells Inputs from economic factors of production (natural, social, financial, human and built
us capital) which in combination determine economic output -- which in a sustainable
economy, are reinvested to maintain and enhance the economy. Economic growth also
provides increased tax revenue to pay down public debt.
Nova Scotia has maintained GDP growth during the global economic downturn.
Rank: 9th among provinces for second year in a row.
Trend: Generally improving, though below the national growth rate.

Net financial debt, balance sheet of federal, provincial and territorial general and local
Data governments, annual, Statistics Canada (2008) CANSIM Table 385-0014.
Gross domestic product (GDP), expenditure-based, provincial economic accounts, Statistics
Description of Net financial debt divided by GDP.
Most recent 2008 (Data source will be discontinued and recast after 2012 to be standardized by the
Rationale for High levels of debt reduce government funds available for investment in the capitals and
selection may lead to reduced programs and services, increased taxes and higher borrowing costs for
Nova Scotia’s level of public debt remains problematic, especially in light of an aging What it tells
us population and increasing health care costs.
Level: More than double the Canadian average.
Rank: Second last in the country, ahead of Quebec.

Description of Compiled dollar value of timber stocks, known oil and gas reserves, and unmined
– Most recent 2010 Series has not been continued. New Data sources are needed.
Rationale for Natural resources are a major input into GDP growth in Canada. When comparing
economic performance, it is important to consider the value of the province’s natural selection
What it tells Nova Scotia has relatively low levels of natural resource wealth (in timber, oil and gas and
us minerals) compared to Canada as a whole.
Level: $8,818 per person, below the Canadian average of $31,362/person.
Rank: 8th among provinces in growth in 2010.
Trend: Generally increasing, though ten-year growth is 10th ranked among provinces.

Electricity generated from fossil fuels per capita.
Electricity generated from fossil fuels, annual (gigawatt hours), Statistics Canada (2008),
Estimates of population, annual, Statistics Canada (2009), CANSIM Table 051-0001.
Description of Based on Electric Power Thermal Generating Station Fuel Consumption divided by
Rationale for Electricity generation represents an unusually high portion of total per capital fossil fuel
selection usage in Nova Scotia compared to most other provinces. Monitoring this indicator will
capture the impact of efforts to develop renewable sources of energy for electrical
generation and improve efficiency of energy use. Reduced use of fossil fuel will help
protect electricity consumers from oil price shocks and contribute to reduced air pollution
What it tells Electricity generation in Nova Scotia dipped in 2006 as a result of the temporary Stora
us closure in Port Hawkesbury. Overall usage is on the decline in the three years since 2007 .
Level: More than twice the Canadian average.
Rank: Nova Scotia is 3rd highest in the country, better than Saskatchewan (#2), and Alberta
Trend: Heavy reliance on fossil fuels to date has limited Nova Scotia to only modest
improvements. Trend may be more promising with impending hydro power on the horizon.
Electricty Generated from Fossil Fuels, per capita
Total primary and secondary demand (in terajoules), Statistics Canada CANSIM Table 128-
Gross Domestic Product, expenditure based -- CANSIM Table 384-0002 (Prices in chained
Description of The summation of the energy usage in mining and oil and gas extraction, manufacturing,
Data forestry, construction, transportation, agriculture, residential, public administration and
Rationale for Energy productivity measures the degree to which the economy is reliant on energy, or the
selection relative energy efficiency of the economy (in terms of total demanded energy, primary and
secondary, in all sectors). Higher GDP/terajoule figures indicate higher energy productivity.
2005 is the baseline year for Federal Climate Change reduction goals, for which energy
productivity will be a key driver.
What it tells Nova Scotia is middle of the road compared with other provinces in energy efficiency, and
us appears to be on an improving trend.
Level: $230,100 per terajoule, 13.2% higher than the Canadian average.
Rank: 4th among provinces. For the past seven years, NS has been ranked in top half.
Trend: Improving. Level is highest on record, with growth rate of 28.2% in the last decade
(only BC has grown by more, percentage wise).
